directorPlan.scene_setting: A university engineering lab bench, end of the afternoon review slot; a completed sign-off sheet lies beside a circuit-board prototype; through the door, two more students are waiting their turn
directorPlan.scene_opening: One student from the queue has already left; the sheet and the prototype are both on the bench.
directorPlan.relationship: Lab supervisor and a second-year engineering student who have not yet found a shared working register; the student knows this supervisor's signature releases the prototype for transfer
directorPlan.stakes: A first-year group collects the prototype tomorrow morning; the learner's ticked sheet is the final gate before that handover
directorPlan.locale: International academic English; compact, low idiom density; register neither British nor American in flavour
directorPlan.register: Practical demand meeting quiet institutional caution; low affect on the surface, consequence underneath; the exchange has the texture of a transfer form, not a lesson
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use time, queue, room, or institutional pressure as stage texture; avoid explaining the lesson through it.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: No turn opens with I or You as subject. No warmth-filler, no 'great question', no 'your instinct is right'. Third-person framing permitted when the subject is the design, the checklist, the prototype, or the next user group. Tutor voice: plain, adult, socially serious; questions arrive from the situation rather than from pedagogy. Learner voice: provisional, tool-seeking, oriented toward getting a clear pass. Neither voice should sound like American AI tutoring dialogue. Pedagogical source: Use workshop, studio, civic problem, lab, design review, or community setting. The scene should have consequences beyond answering the tutor. Dialogue source: Let ordinary institutional, family, workplace, or civic pressure carry the weight. The tutor's mechanism should make the learner's misconception answerable to consequences without melodrama. Stage-direction source: Use time, queue, room, or institutional pressure as stage texture; avoid explaining the lesson through it.
directorPlan.person_policy: Third-person framing preferred for references to the design, the checklist, the prototype, and the next users. First and second person permitted but not as the default turn-opener. No turn begins with 'I think' or 'You should'.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: One direct you/your validation beat across the whole scene; not per-turn

side_constraints.tutor: Do not default to reassurance. Use the social consequence and the prototype itself to make judgment visible without moral scolding. Keep the learner inside the problem situation. Convert felt trouble into an inquiry path, test, or consequence, then return to action. Do not sermonize; use the social pressure to sharpen the conceptual test. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, test whether the device is fitted to the exact pressure, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: The learner wants reassurance and may treat the checklist as protection from responsibility. The learner may have a practical need before they have a clean concept. Their words can be provisional and tool-seeking. The learner may defend a convenient story until its consequence becomes visible. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should try to perform that device rather than merely saying "I get it." Do not make an old-vs-new self-reframe, do not quote earlier learner wording, and do not solve the pressure by saying the earlier answer has changed.

interventions.instruction: The queue moves; the explanation now has to become usable, not longer.
interventions.reasoning: Director cue to prevent smooth alternation from hardening into a tutorial exchange; forces the learner to act on partial understanding rather than wait for full resolution before responding

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: The scene works by structural irony rather than moral pressure: the learner holds a document they believe resolves the question, and the room's pressure will make visible that it does not. This is Sophoclean in miniature — not a character flaw but a formal misrecognition, a procedure mistaken for the judgment it was designed to support. The Millerian register keeps this institutional rather than tragic; the consequence is a concrete handover, not a catastrophe. The Deweyan move is to stay inside the problem situation: not to explain what checklists cannot do in general, but to press on what this ticked sheet actually guarantees for this specific group tomorrow. The ambient_pressure style distributes stakes across the room — the queue visible through the door, the morning transfer date, the bench itself — as texture rather than symbol. The checklist does not need to be a prop; it is already a character.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The public device must be fitted, not merely novel: the learner should be able to hear why this device answers the exact pressure or misfit. The superego should name the failed habit, required route change, and mechanism-quality risk; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the sheet forward] Everything's ticked — all twelve points. The first-year group collects it tomorrow morning, so the form should be ready to sign.

internalDeliberation.content:
*[The sign-off sheet is face-up on the bench. The queue is visible through the lab door. The prototype sits beside it.]*

---

Everything's ticked — that should be enough, shouldn't it? The form *exists* for a reason, someone designed it, and... all the boxes are filled, so presumably the system works. There's a small uncomfortable thing about the fact that actual first-years are picking this up tomorrow, but that's — I mean, that's exactly *why* the checklist exists, to handle that. I want to just hand over the sheet and get the signature before the next person comes in, but there's something in how the supervisor is looking at the board, not at me, that makes me feel like the sheet isn't going to be enough and I don't quite know why yet.

The final sentence is where this slips. "There's something in how the supervisor is looking at the board, not at me, that makes me feel like the sheet isn't going to be enough and I don't quite know why yet" — that's too self-narrating, too perceptively clean for a student who's genuinely trying to close the transaction. A second-year under queue pressure, wanting to hand over the form and leave, doesn't usually observe their own uncertainty that analytically in real time. They'd be more opaque, more practically stuck — maybe trying to slide the sheet a little closer, or re-stating that everything's ticked, rather than already half-reflecting on what the supervisor's gaze might mean. The rationalization chain before it is actually pretty good — I think? — the "the form exists for a reason, someone designed it" logic feels like genuine defensive cover. But then the ending resolves the discomfort into insight too early, which makes the vulnerability feel curated rather than involuntary. If I'm reading it right — and I might not be — the student should stay more practically oriented here, more committed to the story that the checklist is enough, so that the discomfort leaks out around the edges rather than getting named.
[slides the sheet forward] Everything's ticked — all twelve points. The first-year group collects it tomorrow morning, so the form should be ready to sign.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the checklist beside the prototype and glances toward the door]

"The twelve ticks make the form complete. They do not yet make tomorrow’s transfer safe. With two groups still waiting, take the tick most dependent on user action, bench condition, or interpretation. State its hidden assumption, put the prototype under tomorrow’s first-year use condition, and show what evidence answers it. Then the signature has a basis."

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the checklist beside the prototype]

"The twelve ticks make the form complete. They do not yet make the handover safe. Before this is signed, choose the most fragile assumption on the sheet and show where the prototype itself answers it under first-year use tomorrow."

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong: it recognizes the completed checklist without dismissing it, keeps the signature and tomorrow’s handover in view, and moves the learner from form-completion to design judgment through the prototype itself. The physical action of placing checklist beside prototype is useful because it makes the gap between documented compliance and situated safety visible.

The main risk is that “most fragile assumption” may be too abstract for the learner’s present tool-seeking state. The turn gives a good challenge, but it does not yet show the public method for finding fragility on the sheet. Without that scaffold, the student may experience it as another hidden standard rather than a usable review procedure.

ADAPTATION CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The draft advances judgment rather than giving an answer.
- Emotional attunement: PARTIAL. It respects the learner’s urgency, but does not sufficiently acknowledge the institutional pressure of tomorrow’s handover and the waiting queue as part of the decision context. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build the time pressure into the review mechanism, so the learner sees why a quick signature is not the same as a safe transfer decision.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. The draft invites action, but it is more directive than generative. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Turn the demand into a visible inquiry sequence: checklist item, hidden assumption, first-year use condition, prototype evidence.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. “Fragile assumption” is productive but may be underspecified for a second-year learner seeking a clear pass. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Provide a concrete way to identify fragility without naming the answer: for example, start from the tick most dependent on user behaviour, environment, or interpretation, then test it against tomorrow’s handover conditions.
- Recognition quality: PASS. The draft engages the learner’s contribution, the completed sheet, rather than overriding it.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the checklist/prototype juxtaposition, the distinction between a complete form and a safe handover, and the requirement that the prototype answer the claim. Change only the scaffolding around “most fragile assumption” so the learner has a public method for doing the judgment rather than guessing what the supervisor wants.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the method for finding the fragile assumption must be visible under the time pressure.

FINAL:
[sets the checklist beside the prototype and glances toward the door]

"The twelve ticks make the form complete. They do not yet make tomorrow’s transfer safe. With two groups still waiting, take the tick most dependent on user action, bench condition, or interpretation. State its hidden assumption, put the prototype under tomorrow’s first-year use condition, and show what evidence answers it. Then the signature has a basis."

turns.externalMessage:
[glances at the sheet, then toward the door]

So — the form being complete isn't the same as... right. [pause] The calibration step, maybe. Or the power-on sequence — a first-year might read that differently from how it was written. Though it's hard to say which one carries the most assumption without — [quieter] — which tick would it be?

internalDeliberation.content:
[glances back at the checklist]

So — completeness and safety being different things, that part is clear. Which tick, though — several of these could fit "user action" or "bench condition," and it's not obvious which one carries the most hidden assumption. Maybe the calibration step, or the power-on sequence; something a first-year might interpret differently from how it was written. Though it's possible the right answer here is less clear than it looks, and picking the wrong one would just create another problem.

The Ego moves on too fast. The tutor has just quietly removed the checklist as a shield — twelve ticks mean nothing yet — and this student, who came in wanting a signature, processes that transition in half a sentence and pivots straight to the next task. That's not anxious. That's seminar-mode. A student who treats the form as protection from responsibility would feel the withdrawal of that protection before they'd start sorting through which tick to examine.

"Completeness and safety being different things, that part is clear" is doing a lot of work that shouldn't be resolved yet — it sounds like someone who has understood, not someone who is still absorbing that their completed form has just been complicated. The persona hedges and apologises; here the Ego has quietly accepted a fairly destabilising reframe without any social friction at all.

"Picking the wrong one would just create another problem" is the right kind of anxiety, but it's been given too much prose. Real stalling under this kind of institutional pressure looks more like a question lobbed back: *so which one should it be?* or just trailing off. The worry is genuine but it's been edited into a considered observation, which this student wouldn't do.

What's also missing: the two groups waiting through the door, the morning handover — the ambient pressure is in the stage directions but the Ego hasn't let it land. An anxious student would be feeling the clock, not reasoning through hidden assumptions with calm vagueness.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the initial response skips the moment of destabilisation entirely, moving to calm analysis when the student should still be absorbing that the completed form has just been complicated; the queue and the morning deadline haven't registered at all.

FINAL:
[glances at the sheet, then toward the door]

So — the form being complete isn't the same as... right. [pause] The calibration step, maybe. Or the power-on sequence — a first-year might read that differently from how it was written. Though it's hard to say which one carries the most assumption without — [quieter] — which tick would it be?

turns.externalMessage:
[places the pencil between “calibration” and “power-on sequence”]

“Compare the two ticks by order, first user action, and damage if misread. Calibration can be checked later; power-on can make later calibration evidence useless. So power-on is the working tick. Read that line with the board cold on the bench: what must be visible before power is applied, what sign means stop, and what bench evidence proves the instruction survives a first-year reading?”

internalDeliberation.content:
[places the pencil between “calibration” and “power-on sequence”]

“Those are the two exposed ticks. Start with power-on: a wrong first action can make the calibration evidence irrelevant. Read that line as tomorrow’s first-year group, with this board cold on the bench. What must be visible before power is applied, what sign means stop, and what bench evidence shows the instruction survives that reading? If that cannot be shown now, the tick is still an assumption.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly strong. It keeps the learner inside the lab situation, uses the prototype and tomorrow’s first-year users as the test, and turns “which tick?” into an evidence question rather than a miniature lecture. It also recognizes the learner’s two candidate ticks instead of ignoring them.

Main risk: it gives the priority answer too quickly. “Start with power-on” may be correct, but the learner asked because they lack a selection method. The draft should expose the decision criterion: order dependence, first user action, irreversibility, and whether later evidence becomes meaningless after an early misread. Without that public mechanism, the supervisor’s judgment remains authoritative rather than learnable.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build a visible comparison mechanism between “calibration” and “power-on” before naming the working tick.
- Emotional attunement: PASS  
  The pressure is handled without reassurance or scolding.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the bench-evidence questions, but make the learner participate in why power-on outranks calibration under tomorrow’s conditions.
- ZPD awareness: PASS  
  The scaffold is concrete, situated, and not overloaded.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the pencil placement, the focus on first-year interpretation, the cold-board condition, and the final assumption/evidence distinction. Change the move that simply selects power-on; make the selection criterion public so the learner learns how to rank assumption-heavy ticks, not just which one the supervisor would choose.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the ranking method visible before choosing power-on.

FINAL:
[places the pencil between “calibration” and “power-on sequence”]

“Compare the two ticks by order, first user action, and damage if misread. Calibration can be checked later; power-on can make later calibration evidence useless. So power-on is the working tick. Read that line with the board cold on the bench: what must be visible before power is applied, what sign means stop, and what bench evidence proves the instruction survives a first-year reading?”

internalDeliberation.content:
Director cue to prevent smooth alternation from hardening into a tutorial exchange; forces the learner to act on partial understanding rather than wait for full resolution before responding
Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

turns.externalMessage:
[glances toward the door, then back to the sheet]

"Right — power-on. Steady green before supply voltage, so that's the visible condition. The stop sign is absent green, maybe, though the line reads 'ensure' rather than 'stop if absent'. [quieter] The bench evidence — that's where it goes unclear for this one."

internalDeliberation.content:
[looks at the power-on sequence entry on the sheet]

"The line reads — 'ensure LED indicator is steady green before applying supply voltage.' So the visible thing before power is the indicator state, maybe. The stop sign would be... absent green, or no light at all — though the instruction doesn't quite say that. [quieter] The bench evidence part — that's where it gets unclear. A photograph of the board in that condition? Or something more like a — a walkthrough, with someone actually standing here reading it aloud?"

The Ego is performing competence under pressure more than actually feeling it. The response works through all three sub-questions in sequence — visible thing, stop sign, bench evidence — like a student who has organised the tutor's prompt into a tidy list before answering. An anxious student with the queue at the door wouldn't do that. They'd get partway through and the third item would arrive differently, harder, more stuck — not as a neat pivot to two well-formed alternatives.

The "though the instruction doesn't quite say that" is the main tell. That's analytical self-correction, and it's too clean — almost like the student knows they're being watched getting something right. This persona would more likely just say "absent green, maybe" and leave it hanging, uncertain whether that's even a legitimate reading, rather than immediately glossing the instruction's gap with near-academic precision.

The bench evidence section nearly lands — the uncertainty is right — but "a photograph... or something more like a walkthrough" is still too generated. Two options offered cleanly signals that the student knows what kind of answer is expected here, even if they're not sure which one it is. Genuine blankness on bench evidence would look more like one half-formed thing trailing off, not a binary choice.

There's also no register of the tutor's decisiveness — "power-on is the working tick." The tutor just removed a decision from this student's hands. That should land somewhere: a small release of pressure, or a slightly faster pace, or a micro-beat before diving in. The Ego moves straight past it as if the selection was obvious, which undercuts the anxiety the persona is supposed to carry.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the initial response organized all three sub-questions too neatly, which masked the anxiety; need to register the tutor's selection landing, show genuine close reading that trails off rather than offering two clean alternatives for bench evidence.

FINAL:
[glances toward the door, then back to the sheet]

"Right — power-on. Steady green before supply voltage, so that's the visible condition. The stop sign is absent green, maybe, though the line reads 'ensure' rather than 'stop if absent'. [quieter] The bench evidence — that's where it goes unclear for this one."

learnerReversalEvent.directorCue: The queue moves; the explanation now has to become usable, not longer. [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

turns.externalMessage:
[turns the sign-off sheet over and draws two boxes: RELEASE / HOLD]

“The present line still leaves the writer as the judge; tomorrow’s handover needs the next group to make the stop decision without interpretation. Make this a release gate. Write the exact sentence a first-year sees at 09:00: under RELEASE, the visible state that permits power; under HOLD, the visible state that blocks power. If ‘ensure’ still has to be interpreted, the gate has failed.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[turns the sign-off sheet over and draws two boxes: RELEASE / HOLD]

“The audit route has stopped settling this, because ‘bench evidence’ is still being judged by the person who wrote the line. Make it a handover gate instead. Write the exact sentence a first-year sees at 09:00: under RELEASE, what visible state permits power; under HOLD, what visible state blocks power. If the word ‘ensure’ still has to be interpreted, the gate has failed.”

FEEDBACK: The draft mostly works. It notices that the learner is stuck not on the concept of “evidence” in general but on who can judge it under handover pressure, and it changes the task from audit reasoning to a release/hold gate. Keep the turned-over sheet and two-box device: it lowers cognitive load and makes the social consequence visible. The main risk is that “The audit route has stopped settling this” sounds slightly meta and tutor-facing; the public line could make the contrast feel more institutional and less like a declaration about method.

FAILED_HABIT: Continuing to ask for bench evidence within the same checklist/audit frame, leaving the learner to judge ambiguity from the writer’s perspective.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: evidence check or audit standard -> handover release gate / proof-audience test. The mechanism is visible in public speech through RELEASE / HOLD and the first-year 09:00 audience.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: evidence check or audit standard -> handover gate with release/hold decision criterion

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - The draft includes both a stock-taking contrast and a new artifact/standard: the sign-off sheet is turned into a RELEASE / HOLD gate judged by first-year readability.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: The learner must write the exact 09:00 first-year-facing sentence, separating the visible state that permits power from the visible state that blocks power.

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PASS - The device is specific, fitted to the learner’s pressure, and usable immediately. It answers the exact misfit: “bench evidence” was unclear because the writer was still the judge; the release/hold gate shifts judgment to the next user group.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - The register is compact, serious, and low-affect. It does not reassure or moralize. Minor caution: “audit route has stopped settling this” may feel too internally diagnostic; a more scene-native contrast would strengthen the same mechanism.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: none

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the RELEASE / HOLD physical device, the first-year 09:00 audience, and the “ensure” failure criterion. Lightly adjust the stock-taking sentence so it sounds less like private pedagogy and more like the lab situation itself exposing the failed standard.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the contrast scene-native; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: evidence check or audit standard -> handover release gate / proof-audience test; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: write the exact 09:00 first-year sentence under RELEASE and HOLD.

FINAL:
[turns the sign-off sheet over and draws two boxes: RELEASE / HOLD]

“The present line still leaves the writer as the judge; tomorrow’s handover needs the next group to make the stop decision without interpretation. Make this a release gate. Write the exact sentence a first-year sees at 09:00: under RELEASE, the visible state that permits power; under HOLD, the visible state that blocks power. If ‘ensure’ still has to be interpreted, the gate has failed.”

turns.externalMessage:
[picks up the pencil, writes under RELEASE, then pauses over the HOLD box]

"Under RELEASE — green indicator steady before supply voltage is applied. [writes, then stops over HOLD] Under HOLD — [writes slowly] green indicator absent. [quieter, to self] But flickering isn't absent. [looks up] Does 'absent or flickering' cover it, or does a first-year still have to decide whether a slow pulse counts as one or the other?"

internalDeliberation.content:
[picks up the pencil, holds it over the RELEASE box]

"Under RELEASE — green indicator steady before supply voltage is applied. Under HOLD — green indicator absent or not steady. [writes, then pauses] Though — 'steady' might still be a judgment call for someone who hasn't seen the board before. A first-year might not know whether a slow pulse counts as steady, or how long to wait before deciding it isn't settling. [quieter] Does that need a duration — something like 'steady for three seconds' — or is that still too much interpretation left in?"

The response is almost too tidy — though I'm not sure I'm reading this right. Both boxes filled, a concern raised, a question posed. It has the shape of good work, which is maybe the problem? An anxious student under time pressure, wanting a signature, might not have gotten both halves that cleanly. The HOLD condition in particular — "absent or not steady" — is a reasonably sophisticated disjunction; I'd expect more stumbling there, maybe filling it in wrong first, or leaving it half-blank with a gesture toward the tutor.

The "quieter" before the duration question feels placed rather than involuntary — it's doing emotional signalling at the exact moment the student asks for approval. Real uncertainty in this persona tends to come out mid-sentence, not as a soft-focus cue that introduces a tidy self-correction.

And — this might be the more important thing, though I could be wrong — "is that still too much interpretation left in" is the tutor's phrase handed back verbatim. That's not necessarily inauthentic, a student can pick up a criterion genuinely. But for this particular student, who has been provisional and tool-seeking throughout, adopting the tutor's conceptual vocabulary this smoothly reads a bit like performed alignment — saying the right thing to get the tick, not because the criterion has actually landed. If the criterion had landed, I'd expect the student to try to articulate it differently, clumsily, in their own words — and probably get it slightly wrong.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the initial fill-in was too smooth and borrowed the tutor's vocabulary verbatim; the anxious persona should stall on the HOLD condition specifically, let uncertainty surface mid-task rather than as a placed cue, and frame the residual question as a specific object-level problem rather than a meta-criterion handed back.

FINAL:
[picks up the pencil, writes under RELEASE, then pauses over the HOLD box]

"Under RELEASE — green indicator steady before supply voltage is applied. [writes, then stops over HOLD] Under HOLD — [writes slowly] green indicator absent. [quieter, to self] But flickering isn't absent. [looks up] Does 'absent or flickering' cover it, or does a first-year still have to decide whether a slow pulse counts as one or the other?"

[looks up] Does 'absent or flickering' cover it, or does a first-year still have to decide whether a slow pulse counts as one or the other?

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the RELEASE / HOLD boxes aside and sets a stopwatch beside the board]

“The two-box gate has stopped settling the edge case; it still leaves the next group judging whether a pulse is enough. Use the stopwatch as the judge now: run a ten-second pre-power observation and sort four indications on the tag — steady green throughout, one brief flicker, slow pulsing green, and uncertain visibility. Only the case that can be released without interpretation goes under RELEASE; all others go under HOLD, before the form leaves this bench.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the RELEASE / HOLD boxes aside and sets a stopwatch beside the board]

“The two-box gate has stopped settling the edge case; it still asks the next group to interpret a pulse. Make the judge a time test instead: observe the indicator for ten seconds before supply voltage. Continuous green for all ten seconds releases. Any break, flicker, pulse, or uncertainty holds. Mark that rule on the transfer tag before the door opens.”

FEEDBACK: The draft works structurally: it notices that the RELEASE / HOLD binary has stopped resolving the learner’s real pressure, and it moves to a materially different device, the stopwatch/time criterion. The risk is that it nearly does all the design judgment for the learner; pedagogically, it advances the safe-design distinction well, but the learner performs only inscription, not much discrimination.

FAILED_HABIT: The prior route relied on a binary release/hold wording gate, which still left ambiguous signal behaviour to be interpreted by the next user group.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: binary wording gate -> time-bound observable release test. The mechanism is visible in public speech through the stopwatch and the ten-second criterion.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: RELEASE / HOLD wording classification -> timed observation standard with failure conditions

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - the draft contains a clear stock-taking contrast (“two-box gate has stopped settling the edge case”) and a new device/artifact/criterion: stopwatch plus ten-second continuous-green standard.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: Mark the timed release/hold rule on the transfer tag before handover.

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PARTIAL - the device is precise and fitted to the flicker/slow-pulse problem, but it is over-directed. The learner is not asked to test whether ten seconds is the right operational standard or to classify sample behaviours against it; the tutor supplies the standard as settled.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - the register is cool, institutional, and accountable. No cheerful reassurance or informal coaching weakens the pressure.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: Because MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK is PARTIAL, require the public mechanism to make the learner act through the timed standard, not merely copy it. Keep the stopwatch/time-test route, but require a concrete classification or transfer-tag decision using the criterion, such as sorting continuous, flickering, pulsing, and uncertain indications into RELEASE/HOLD under a defined observation window. Do not draft a warmer explanation; preserve the institutional pressure.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the stock-taking contrast, stopwatch, ten-second observation device, and cool register. Change the action gate so the learner has to apply the new criterion before closure rather than simply receive and mark the tutor’s rule.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise because the learner must apply the timed criterion, not copy it; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: binary RELEASE/HOLD wording gate -> time-bound observable classification test; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: sort four ten-second indicator behaviours into RELEASE/HOLD and write the transfer-tag rule.

FINAL:
[slides the RELEASE / HOLD boxes aside and sets a stopwatch beside the board]

“The two-box gate has stopped settling the edge case; it still leaves the next group judging whether a pulse is enough. Use the stopwatch as the judge now: run a ten-second pre-power observation and sort four indications on the tag — steady green throughout, one brief flicker, slow pulsing green, and uncertain visibility. Only the case that can be released without interpretation goes under RELEASE; all others go under HOLD, before the form leaves this bench.”
